text: We do not say that a man who takes no interest in politics is a man who minds his own business; we say that he has no business here at all.
context: Celebrating Athenian democracy and civic participation during the Peloponnesian War.

text: Our constitution is called a democracy because power is in the hands not of a minority but of the whole people.
context: Defining the radical democracy of Athens.

text: We throw open our city to the world, and never by alien acts exclude foreigners from any opportunity.
context: Praising Athens' openness and cultural generosity.

text: The whole earth is the tomb of famous men; not only are they commemorated by columns and inscriptions in their own country, but in foreign lands there dwells also an unwritten memorial of them, graven not on stone but in the hearts of men.
context: On the lasting legacy of those who died for the city.

text: We cultivate refinement without extravagance and knowledge without effeminacy; wealth we employ more for use than for show.

I would have you day by day fix your eyes upon the greatness of Athens, until you become filled with the love of her; and when you are impressed by the spectacle of her glory, reflect that this empire has been acquired by men who knew their duty and had the courage to do it.
context: Inspiring the living to live up to the sacrifice of the dead.

text: The bravest are surely those who have the clearest vision of what is before them, glory and danger alike, and yet notwithstanding, go out to meet it.
context: Honoring the courage of Athenian soldiers.

text: We are lovers of the beautiful, yet simple in our tastes, and we cultivate the mind without loss of manliness.
context: The famous characterization of Athenian culture.

text: Our city is thrown open to the world; we never expel a foreigner or prevent him from seeing or learning anything of which the secret if revealed to an enemy might profit him.
context: Praising transparency and confidence in Athenian institutions.

text: Make up your minds that happiness depends on being free, and freedom depends on being courageous.
context: Direct call to the values that sustain democracy.

text: We do not copy our neighbors, but are an example to them.
It is only the love of honor that never grows old; and honor it is, not gain, as some would have it, that rejoices the heart of age and helplessness.
context: On what truly motivates noble action.

text: The sacrifice which they collectively made was individually repaid to them; for they received again each one for himself a praise which grows not old, and the noblest of all sepulchres — I speak not of that in which their remains are laid, but of that in which their glory survives.
context: The immortality of reputation through noble death.

text: We are free to live exactly as we please, and yet we are always ready to face any danger.
context: Balancing personal freedom with civic readiness.

text: Our ordinary citizens, though occupied with the pursuits of industry, are still fair judges of public matters; for we do not regard the individual who takes no part in these duties as unambitious but as useless.
context: Defense of the politically engaged citizen.

text: In the hour of trial Athens alone among her contemporaries is superior to the report of her.
context: Claiming that reality exceeds even the reputation of the city.

text: We have compelled every sea and every land to open a path for our courage.
The Athens that I have celebrated is only what the heroism of these and their like have made her.
context: Crediting the fallen with the city's greatness.

text: Such is the Athens for which these men, in the assertion of their resolve not to lose her, nobly fought and died.
context: Summarizing the stakes of the war.

text: The whole world is the sepulchre of illustrious men; their epitaphs are to be found not only in their own land but in foreign countries, not graven on stone but living in the hearts and minds of men.
context: Variation on the theme of undying reputation.

text: We are the school of Hellas.
context: Claiming Athens as the educator of Greece.

text: The empire we have acquired is the greatest that has ever existed.
context: On the extent of Athenian power.

text: We have left behind us imperishable monuments of our power.
The admiration of the present and succeeding ages will be ours.
We have forced every sea and every land to be the highway of our daring.
We have everywhere planted eternal memorials of our friendship and of our enmity.
The whole earth is the sepulchre of famous men.
Their story is not graven only on stone over their native earth, but lives on far away, without visible symbol, woven into the stuff of other men's lives.

For the whole earth is the sepulchre of famous men; and their story is not graven only on stone over their native earth, but lives on far away, without visible symbol, woven into the stuff of other men's lives.
The Athens that I have celebrated is only what the heroism of these and their like have made her.
Such is the Athens for which these men, in the assertion of their resolve not to lose her, nobly fought and died.
context: Tying the dead to the city.
